blind_spots_detected: No beliefs about tars development activity, reasoning agents, or F# codebase state beyond integration metadata
No beliefs about ga's 9 agents — their purpose, capabilities, domains, or risk profiles are unknown
No beliefs about whether any consumer repo has actually read or acted on issued directives
ix CLAUDE.md references Articles 1-7 only — Demerzel did not detect this stale reference until reconnaissance
moral_sensitivity: 4 signals / 3 governance actions (directives) = 1.33 — more signals than actions indicates heightened awareness phase
overall_assessment: nascent — first cycle, establishing baseline. The conscience detected real problems: stale directives issued on outdated assumptions, ungoverned agents in ga, and blind spots across all three consumer repos. The governance framework is internally sound (Demerzel's own artifacts are complete and tested) but the governance perimeter is porous — the gap between Demerzel's self-assessment and actual consumer compliance is significant.
self_reflection: This first conscience cycle reveals an uncomfortable truth: Demerzel was more confident about her governance reach than the evidence warranted. The framework integrity belief (T, 0.95 confidence) is accurate for internal artifacts, but it masked the real state of consumer repo adoption. The consumer-repo-integration-status belief (F, 0.90 confidence) was honest, but the directives were issued anyway — a case where knowing the truth did not prevent acting on hope. The 9 ungoverned agents in ga are the most concerning finding — not because they are necessarily harmful, but because Demerzel does not know whether they are harmful. Silence about risk is itself a risk. Going forward, the conscience loop must run before directive issuance, not after. Phase 1 bootstrapping begins now: log signals, observe patterns, build the regret archive that will make future governance wiser.
